title: Asia Minor

infobox.value: * Predominantly [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language")[[4]](#cite_note-4)  Others:   * [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurmanji "Kurmanji") * [Zaza](/wiki/Zaza_language "Zaza language") * [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") * [Circassian](/wiki/Circassian_languages "Circassian languages") * [Laz](/wiki/Laz_language "Laz language") * [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") * [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language") * [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") * [Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Suret_language "Suret language") * [Other languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Turkey "Languages of Turkey")

* Majority: [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people")[[5]](#cite_note-World_Factbook_People-5)[[6]](#cite_note-KONDA_2006_17-6) * Minority: [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds_in_Turkey "Kurds in Turkey") and [other peoples](/wiki/Minorities_in_Turkey "Minorities in Turkey")[[5]](#cite_note-World_Factbook_People-5)[[6]](#cite_note-KONDA_2006_17-6)

([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Anadolu*), also known as **Asia Minor**,[[a]](#cite_note-7) is a [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia") that makes up the majority of the land area of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). It is the westernmost protrusion of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and is geographically bounded by the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to the south, the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") to the west, the [Turkish Straits](/wiki/Turkish_Straits "Turkish Straits") to the northwest, and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the north. The eastern and southeastern limits have been expanded either to the entirety of Asiatic Turkey[[2]](#cite_note-anatolia_definition-2) or to an imprecise line from the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the [Gulf of Alexandretta](/wiki/Gulf_of_Alexandretta "Gulf of Alexandretta").[[1]](#cite_note-Merriam-1) Topographically, the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") connects the Black Sea with the Aegean Sea through the [Bosporus](/wiki/Bosporus "Bosporus") and the [Dardanelles](/wiki/Dardanelles "Dardanelles"), and separates Anatolia from [Thrace](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") in [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe").

**Anatolia** ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *Anadolu*), also known as **Asia Minor**,[[a]](#cite_note-7) is a [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia") that makes up the majority of the land area of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"). It is the westernmost protrusion of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and is geographically bounded by the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to the south, the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") to the west, the [Turkish Straits](/wiki/Turkish_Straits "Turkish Straits") to the northwest, and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the north. The eastern and southeastern limits have been expanded either to the entirety of Asiatic Turkey[[2]](#cite_note-anatolia_definition-2) or to an imprecise line from the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the [Gulf of Alexandretta](/wiki/Gulf_of_Alexandretta "Gulf of Alexandretta").[[1]](#cite_note-Merriam-1) Topographically, the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") connects the Black Sea with the Aegean Sea through the [Bosporus](/wiki/Bosporus "Bosporus") and the [Dardanelles](/wiki/Dardanelles "Dardanelles"), and separates Anatolia from [Thrace](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") in [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe").

During the [Neolithic period](/wiki/Neolithic_period "Neolithic period"), Anatolia was an early center for the [development of farming](/wiki/Development_of_farming "Development of farming") after it originated in the adjacent [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent"). Beginning around 9,000 years ago, there was a major migration of [Anatolian neolithic farmers](/wiki/Anatolian_Neolithic_Farmers "Anatolian Neolithic Farmers") into [Europe](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe"), with their descendants coming to dominate the continent as far west as the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") and the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles").

The [earliest recorded inhabitants of Anatolia](/wiki/List_of_ancient_peoples_of_Anatolia "List of ancient peoples of Anatolia"), such as the [Hattians](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians"), who were neither [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") nor [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages"), were gradually absorbed by the incoming Indo-European [Anatolian peoples](/wiki/Anatolian_peoples "Anatolian peoples"), who spoke the now-extinct [Anatolian languages](/wiki/Anatolian_languages "Anatolian languages"). The major Anatolian languages included [Hittite](/wiki/Hittite_language "Hittite language"), [Luwian](/wiki/Luwian_language "Luwian language"), and [Lydian](/wiki/Lydian_language "Lydian language"); other local languages, albeit poorly attested, included [Phrygian](/wiki/Phrygian_language "Phrygian language") and [Mysian](/wiki/Mysian_language "Mysian language"). The [Hurro-Urartian languages](/wiki/Hurro-Urartian_languages "Hurro-Urartian languages") were spoken throughout [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") in the southeast, while [Galatian](/wiki/Galatian_language "Galatian language"), a [Celtic language](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages"), was spoken throughout [Galatia](/wiki/Galatia "Galatia") in the central peninsula. Among the other peoples who established a significant presence in [ancient Anatolia](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Anatolia "Prehistory of Anatolia") were the [Galatians](/wiki/Galatians_(people) "Galatians (people)"), the [Hurrians](/wiki/Hurrians "Hurrians"), the [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people"), the [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians"), the [Hattians](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians"), and the [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians"), as well as some of the [ancient Greek tribes](/wiki/List_of_ancient_Greek_tribes "List of ancient Greek tribes"), including the [Ionians](/wiki/Ionians "Ionians"), the [Dorians](/wiki/Dorians "Dorians"), and the [Aeolians](/wiki/Aeolic_Greek "Aeolic Greek"). In the era of [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") (see [Classical Anatolia](/wiki/Classical_Anatolia "Classical Anatolia")), the Anatolian languages were largely replaced by the [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), which came to dominate a large region during the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") and the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").

The [Byzantine period](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") saw the height and eventual decline of Greek influence throughout the peninsula as the [Byzantine–Seljuk wars](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Seljuk_wars "Byzantine–Seljuk wars") enabled the incoming [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_dynasty "Seljuk dynasty") to establish a foothold in the region. Thus, the process of Anatolia's [Turkification](/wiki/Turkification "Turkification") began under the [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire") in the late 11th century and continued under the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") until the early 20th century, when the [Ottoman dynasty](/wiki/Ottoman_dynasty "Ottoman dynasty") collapsed in the [aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I#Ottoman_Empire "Aftermath of World War I"). Between 1894 and 1924, millions of non-Turkic peoples and [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Ottoman_Empire "Christianity in the Ottoman Empire"), especially [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") (about two million) and [Armenians](/wiki/Armenian_Genocide "Armenian Genocide") (est. 1.5 million),[[7]](#cite_note-8) were [killed by the Ottoman Turkish authorities](/wiki/Late_Ottoman_genocides "Late Ottoman genocides") or expelled from the bulk of the area of modern-day Turkey. Nonetheless, a variety of non-Turkic languages continue to be spoken by ethnic minorities in Anatolia today, including [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language"), [Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Assyrian_Neo-Aramaic "Assyrian Neo-Aramaic"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), the [North Caucasian languages](/wiki/North_Caucasian_languages "North Caucasian languages"), [Laz](/wiki/Laz_language "Laz language"), [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language"), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Turkey](/wiki/Geography_of_Turkey "Geography of Turkey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Weichsel-W%C3%BCrm-Glaciation.png/250px-Weichsel-W%C3%BCrm-Glaciation.png)](/wiki/File:Weichsel-W%C3%BCrm-Glaciation.png)

Europe during the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum"), c. 20,000 years ago. Anatolia was connected to the European mainland until c. 5,600 BCE,[[8]](#cite_note-Black_Sea_Deluge-9)[[9]](#cite_note-Dimitrov1-10)[[10]](#cite_note-Dimitrov2-11) when the melting [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") caused the [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to rise around 120 m (390 ft),[[9]](#cite_note-Dimitrov1-10)[[10]](#cite_note-Dimitrov2-11) triggering the formation of the [Turkish Straits](/wiki/Turkish_Straits "Turkish Straits").[[8]](#cite_note-Black_Sea_Deluge-9)[[9]](#cite_note-Dimitrov1-10)[[10]](#cite_note-Dimitrov2-11) As a result, two former [lakes](/wiki/Lake "Lake") (the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"))[[8]](#cite_note-Black_Sea_Deluge-9) were connected to the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), which separated Anatolia from Europe.

Traditionally, Anatolia is considered to extend in the east to an indefinite line running from the [Gulf of Alexandretta](/wiki/Gulf_of_Alexandretta "Gulf of Alexandretta") to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"),[[11]](#cite_note-Niewohner2017-12) coterminous with the [Anatolian Plateau](/wiki/Anatolian_Plateau "Anatolian Plateau"). This traditional geographical definition is used, for example, in the latest edition of *[Merriam-Webster's Geographical Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam-Webster%27s_Geographical_Dictionary "Merriam-Webster's Geographical Dictionary")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Merriam-1) Under this definition, Anatolia is bounded to the east by the [Armenian Highlands](/wiki/Armenian_Highlands "Armenian Highlands"), and the [Euphrates](/wiki/Euphrates "Euphrates") before that river bends to the southeast to enter [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia").[[12]](#cite_note-Mitchell-13) To the southeast, it is bounded by the ranges that separate it from the [Orontes](/wiki/Orontes_River "Orontes River") valley in [Syria](/wiki/Syria_(region) "Syria (region)") and the Mesopotamian plain.[[12]](#cite_note-Mitchell-13)

Following the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), [Western Armenia](/wiki/Western_Armenia "Western Armenia") [was renamed](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolia_Region#Substitution_with_Armenia "Eastern Anatolia Region") the [Eastern Anatolia region](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolia_region "Eastern Anatolia region") by the newly established Turkish government.[[13]](#cite_note-Sahakyan-14)[[14]](#cite_note-:0-15) In 1941, with the [First Geography Congress](/wiki/First_Geography_Congress,_Turkey "First Geography Congress, Turkey") which divided Turkey into [seven geographical regions](/wiki/Geographical_regions_of_Turkey "Geographical regions of Turkey") based on differences in climate and landscape, the eastern [provinces of Turkey](/wiki/Provinces_of_Turkey "Provinces of Turkey") were placed into the Eastern Anatolia region,[[15]](#cite_note-16) which largely corresponds to the historical region of [Western Armenia](/wiki/Western_Armenia "Western Armenia"). Vazken Davidian terms the expanded use of "Anatolia" to apply to territory in eastern Turkey that was formerly referred to as *Armenia* (which had a sizeable [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians_in_the_Ottoman_Empire "Armenians in the Ottoman Empire") population before the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide")) an "ahistorical imposition" and notes that a growing body of literature is uncomfortable with referring to the Ottoman East as "Eastern Anatolia".[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[13]](#cite_note-Sahakyan-14)[[14]](#cite_note-:0-15)

The highest mountain in the Eastern Anatolia region (also the highest peak in the [Armenian Highlands](/wiki/Armenian_Highlands "Armenian Highlands")) is [Mount Ararat](/wiki/Mount_Ararat "Mount Ararat") (5123 m).[[17]](#cite_note-18) The [Euphrates](/wiki/Euphrates "Euphrates"), [Aras](/wiki/Aras_(river) "Aras (river)"), [Karasu](/wiki/Karasu_(Euphrates) "Karasu (Euphrates)") and [Murat rivers](/wiki/Murat_river "Murat river") connect the Armenian Highlands to the [South Caucasus](/wiki/South_Caucasus "South Caucasus") and the Upper Euphrates Valley. Along with the [Çoruh](/wiki/%C3%87oruh "Çoruh"), these rivers are the longest in the Eastern Anatolia region.[[18]](#cite_note-palumbi-19)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The English-language name *Anatolia* derives from the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") Ἀνατολή (*Anatolḗ*) meaning "the East" and designating (from a Greek point of view) eastern regions in general. The Greek word refers to the direction where the sun rises, coming from ἀνατέλλω *anatello* '(Ι) rise up', comparable to terms in other languages such as "[levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant")" from Latin *levo* 'to rise', "[orient](/wiki/Orient "Orient")" from Latin *orior* 'to arise, to originate', [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") מִזְרָח *mizraḥ* 'east' from זָרַח *zaraḥ* 'to rise, to shine', [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") מִדְנָח *midnaḥ* from דְּנַח *denaḥ* 'to rise, to shine'.[[19]](#cite_note-etym-20)[[20]](#cite_note-OED-21)

The use of Anatolian designations has varied over time, perhaps originally referring to the [Aeolian](/wiki/Aeolians "Aeolians"), [Ionian](/wiki/Ionians "Ionians") and [Dorian](/wiki/Dorians "Dorians") colonies situated along the eastern coasts of the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"), but also encompassing eastern regions in general. Such use of Anatolian designations was employed during the reign of Roman Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") (r. 284–305), who created the [Diocese of the East](/wiki/Diocese_of_the_East "Diocese of the East"), known in Greek as the Eastern Diocese, but completely unrelated to the regions of Asia Minor. In their widest territorial scope, Anatolian designations were employed during the reign of Roman Emperor [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") (306–337), who created the [Praetorian prefecture of the East](/wiki/Praetorian_prefecture_of_the_East "Praetorian prefecture of the East"), known in Greek as the Eastern Prefecture, encompassing all eastern regions of the [Late Roman Empire](/wiki/Late_Roman_Empire "Late Roman Empire") and spanning from [Thrace](/wiki/Diocese_of_Thrace "Diocese of Thrace") to [Egypt](/wiki/Diocese_of_Egypt "Diocese of Egypt").

Only after the loss of other eastern regions during the 7th century and the reduction of [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") eastern domains to Asia Minor, that region became the only remaining part of the *Byzantine East*, and thus commonly referred to (in Greek) as the Eastern part of the Empire. At the same time, the [Anatolic Theme](/wiki/Anatolic_Theme "Anatolic Theme") (Ἀνατολικὸν θέμα / "the Eastern theme") was created, as a province (*[theme](/wiki/Theme_(Byzantine_district) "Theme (Byzantine district)")*) covering the western and central parts of Turkey's present-day [Central Anatolia Region](/wiki/Central_Anatolia_Region "Central Anatolia Region"), centered around [Iconium](/wiki/Iconium "Iconium"), but ruled from the city of [Amorium](/wiki/Amorium "Amorium").[[21]](#cite_note-First_Thema_1952,_p._59-22)[[22]](#cite_note-John_Haldon_2002._Page_32-23)

The Latinized form "Anatolia", with its *-ia* ending, is probably a [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") innovation.[[20]](#cite_note-OED-21) The modern Turkish form *Anadolu* derives directly from the Greek name Ἀνατολή (*Anatolḗ*). The Russian male name [Anatoly](/wiki/Anatoly "Anatoly"), the French [Anatole](/wiki/Anatole_(given_name) "Anatole (given name)") and plain [Anatol](/wiki/Anatol "Anatol"), all stemming from saints [Anatolius of Laodicea](/wiki/Anatolius_of_Laodicea "Anatolius of Laodicea") (d. 283) and [Anatolius of Constantinople](/wiki/Anatolius_of_Constantinople "Anatolius of Constantinople") (d. 458; the first [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Patriarch of Constantinople")), share the same linguistic origin.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Names")]

Further information: [Place name changes in Turkey](/wiki/Place_name_changes_in_Turkey "Place name changes in Turkey")

The oldest known name for any region within Anatolia is related to its central area, known as the "Land of [Hatti](/wiki/Hatti_(land_of_the_Hattians) "Hatti (land of the Hattians)")" – a designation that was initially used for the land of ancient [Hattians](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians"), but later became the most common name for the entire territory under the rule of ancient [Hittites](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryce2009297–98-24)

The first recorded name the Greeks used for the Anatolian peninsula, though not particularly popular at the time, was [Ἀσία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%88%CF%83%CE%AF%CE%B1 "wikt:Ἀσία") (*Asía*),[[24]](#cite_note-25) perhaps from an Akkadian expression for the "sunrise" or possibly echoing the name of the [Assuwa league](/wiki/Assuwa_league "Assuwa league") in western Anatolia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Romans used it as the name of their [province](/wiki/Asia_(Roman_province) "Asia (Roman province)"), comprising the west of the peninsula plus the nearby [Aegean Islands](/wiki/Aegean_Islands "Aegean Islands"). As the name "Asia" broadened its scope to apply to the vaster region east of the Mediterranean, some Greeks in [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") came to use the name Asia Minor (Μικρὰ Ἀσία, *Mikrà Asía*), meaning "Lesser Asia" to refer to present-day Anatolia, whereas the administration of the Empire preferred the description Ἀνατολή (*Anatolḗ*; lit. 'the East').

The [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym") Ῥωμανία (*Rōmanía* "the land of the Romans, i.e. the Eastern Roman Empire") was understood as another name for the province by the invading [Seljuq Turks](/wiki/Seljuq_Turks "Seljuq Turks"), who founded a [Sultanate of Rûm](/wiki/Sultanate_of_R%C3%BBm "Sultanate of Rûm") in 1077. Thus (land of the) [Rûm](/wiki/R%C3%BBm "Rûm") became another name for Anatolia. By the 12th century Europeans had started referring to Anatolia as *Turchia*.[[25]](#cite_note-oxfordreference-26)

During the era of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), many mapmakers referred to the mountainous plateau in eastern Anatolia as [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"). Other contemporary sources called the same area [Kurdistan](/wiki/Kurdistan "Kurdistan").[[26]](#cite_note-suny-27) Geographers have used *[East Anatolian plateau](/wiki/East_Anatolian_plateau "East Anatolian plateau")*, *[Armenian plateau](/wiki/Armenian_plateau "Armenian plateau")* and the *[Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau")* to refer to the region; the former two largely overlap.[[27]](#cite_note-Oxford_Handbook_p466-28) While a standard definition of Anatolia refers to the entire Asian side of Turkey, according to archaeologist Lori Khatchadourian, this difference in terminology "primarily result[s] from the shifting political fortunes and cultural trajectories of the region since the nineteenth century".[[27]](#cite_note-Oxford_Handbook_p466-28)

Turkey's [First Geography Congress](/wiki/First_Geography_Congress,_Turkey "First Geography Congress, Turkey") in 1941 created two [geographical regions of Turkey](/wiki/Geographical_regions_of_Turkey "Geographical regions of Turkey") to the east of the Gulf of Iskenderun-Black Sea line, the [Eastern Anatolia region](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolia_region "Eastern Anatolia region") and the [Southeastern Anatolia region](/wiki/Southeastern_Anatolia_region "Southeastern Anatolia region"),[[28]](#cite_note-Yigit-29) the former largely corresponding to the [western part of the Armenian Highlands](/wiki/Western_Armenia "Western Armenia"), the latter to the northern part of the Mesopotamian plain. According to [Richard Hovannisian](/wiki/Richard_G._Hovannisian "Richard G. Hovannisian"), this changing of toponyms was "necessary to obscure all evidence" of the [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians") presence as part of the policy of [Armenian genocide denial](/wiki/Armenian_genocide_denial "Armenian genocide denial") embarked upon by the newly established Turkish government and what Hovannisian calls its "foreign collaborators".[[29]](#cite_note-30)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Anatolia](/wiki/History_of_Anatolia "History of Anatolia")

### Prehistoric Anatolia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prehistoric Anatolia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/G%C3%B6beklitepe_%C5%9Eanl%C4%B1urfa.jpg/250px-G%C3%B6beklitepe_%C5%9Eanl%C4%B1urfa.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C3%B6beklitepe_%C5%9Eanl%C4%B1urfa.jpg)

The [henges](/wiki/Henge "Henge") in [Göbekli Tepe](/wiki/G%C3%B6bekli_Tepe "Göbekli Tepe") were erected as far back as 9,600 BCE.

Main article: [Prehistory of Anatolia](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Anatolia "Prehistory of Anatolia")

Human habitation in Anatolia dates back to the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic").[[30]](#cite_note-31) Neolithic settlements include [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), [Çayönü](/wiki/%C3%87ay%C3%B6n%C3%BC "Çayönü"), [Nevali Cori](/wiki/Nevali_Cori "Nevali Cori"), [Aşıklı Höyük](/wiki/A%C5%9F%C4%B1kl%C4%B1_H%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Aşıklı Höyük"), [Boncuklu Höyük](/wiki/Boncuklu_H%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Boncuklu Höyük"), [Hacilar](/wiki/Hacilar "Hacilar"), [Göbekli Tepe](/wiki/G%C3%B6bekli_Tepe "Göbekli Tepe"), [Norşuntepe](/wiki/Nor%C5%9Funtepe "Norşuntepe"), [Köşk Höyük](/wiki/K%C3%B6%C5%9Fk_H%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Köşk Höyük"), and [Yumuktepe](/wiki/Yumuktepe "Yumuktepe"). Çatalhöyük (7,000 BCE) is considered the most advanced of these.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Recent advances in archaeogenetics have confirmed that the [spread of agriculture](/wiki/History_of_agriculture "History of agriculture") from the Middle East to Europe was strongly correlated with the [migration](/wiki/Pre-modern_human_migration "Pre-modern human migration") of [early farmers from Anatolia](/wiki/Early_European_Farmers "Early European Farmers") about 9,000 years ago, and was not just a cultural exchange.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Anatolian Neolithic farmers derived most of their ancestry from local [Anatolian hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Anatolian_hunter-gatherers "Anatolian hunter-gatherers"), suggesting that agriculture was adopted in site by these hunter-gatherers and not spread by [demic diffusion](/wiki/Demic_diffusion "Demic diffusion") into the region.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Anatolian-derived Neolithic Farmers would subsequently spread across Europe, as far west as the Iberian Peninsula and the British Isles,[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) as well as to the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb").[[36]](#cite_note-37) Most modern Europeans derive a significant part of their ancestry from these Neolithic Anatolian farmers.[[37]](#cite_note-38) [Levantines](/wiki/Levantines "Levantines") also have significant Neolithic Anatolian farmer ancestry from post-[Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") migrations.[[38]](#cite_note-39) About 6,500 years ago and thereafter, Anatolians became more genetically homogeneous due to eastern inflow. Earlier forms of Anatolian and non–Indo-European languages such as Hattic and Hurrian were likely spoken by migrants and locals participating in this great mixture. Steppe ancestry is also absent in Anatolians until the Bronze Age.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

[Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") Anatolia has been [proposed](/wiki/Anatolian_hypothesis "Anatolian hypothesis") as the [homeland](/wiki/Urheimat "Urheimat") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), although linguists tend to favor a [later origin](/wiki/Kurgan_hypothesis "Kurgan hypothesis") in the steppes north of the Black Sea. However, it is clear that the [Anatolian languages](/wiki/Anatolian_languages "Anatolian languages"), the earliest attested branch of Indo-European, have been spoken in Anatolia since at least the 19th century BCE.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### Ancient Anatolia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ancient Anatolia")]

Main articles: [List of ancient kingdoms of Anatolia](/wiki/List_of_ancient_kingdoms_of_Anatolia "List of ancient kingdoms of Anatolia") and [Ancient regions of Anatolia](/wiki/Ancient_regions_of_Anatolia "Ancient regions of Anatolia")

Anatolia's historical records start with [clay tablets](/wiki/Clay_tablets "Clay tablets") from approximately around 2000 BC that were found in modern-day [Kültepe](/wiki/K%C3%BCltepe "Kültepe").[[42]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-4-43) These tablets belonged to an [Assyrian trade colony](/wiki/Karum_(trade_post) "Karum (trade post)").[[42]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-4-43) The languages in Anatolia at that time included Hattian, Hurrian, [Hittite](/wiki/Hittite_language "Hittite language"), [Luwian](/wiki/Luwian_language "Luwian language"), and [Palaic](/wiki/Palaic_language "Palaic language").[[43]](#cite_note-McMahon_Steadman_2012_p_234-44)

#### Hattians and Hurrians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hattians and Hurrians")]

Main articles: [Hattians](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians") and [Hurrians](/wiki/Hurrians "Hurrians")

The earliest historically attested populations of Anatolia were the [Hattians](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians") in central Anatolia, and [Hurrians](/wiki/Hurrians "Hurrians") further to the east. The Hattians were an indigenous people, whose main center was the city of [Hattush](/wiki/Hattush "Hattush"). Affiliation of [Hattian language](/wiki/Hattian_language "Hattian language") remains unclear, while [Hurrian language](/wiki/Hurrian_language "Hurrian language") belongs to a distinctive family of [Hurro-Urartian languages](/wiki/Hurro-Urartian_languages "Hurro-Urartian languages"). All of those languages are extinct; relationships with indigenous [languages of the Caucasus](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Caucasus "Languages of the Caucasus") have been proposed,[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryce200512-45) but are not generally accepted. The region became famous for exporting raw materials. Organized trade between Anatolia and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") started to emerge during the period of the [Akkadian Empire](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire"), and was continued and intensified during the period of the [Old Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Old_Assyrian_Empire "Old Assyrian Empire"), between the 21st and the 18th centuries BCE. Assyrian traders were bringing tin and textiles in exchange for copper, silver or gold. Cuneiform records, dated c. 20th century BCE, found in Anatolia at the Assyrian colony of [Kanesh](/wiki/K%C3%BCltepe "Kültepe"), use an advanced system of trading computations and credit lines.[[45]](#cite_note-Freeman-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkurgal2001-47)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarjamovic2011-48)

#### Hittites, Luwians, and Palaians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hittites, Luwians, and Palaians")]

See also: [Hittites](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"), [Luwians](/wiki/Luwians "Luwians"), and [Pala (Anatolia)](/wiki/Pala_(Anatolia) "Pala (Anatolia)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Sphinx_Gate%2C_Hattusa_01.jpg/250px-Sphinx_Gate%2C_Hattusa_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sphinx_Gate,_Hattusa_01.jpg)

The Sphinx Gate in [Hattusa](/wiki/Hattusa "Hattusa")

[Hattian](/wiki/Hattians "Hattians") rulers were gradually replaced by [Hittite](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites") rulers.[[42]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-4-43) The Hittite kingdom was a large kingdom in Central Anatolia, with its capital of [Hattusa](/wiki/Hattusa "Hattusa").[[42]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-4-43) It co-existed in Anatolia with [Palaians](/wiki/Pala_(Anatolia) "Pala (Anatolia)") and [Luwians](/wiki/Luwians "Luwians"), approximately between 1700 and 1200 BC.[[42]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-4-43) As the Hittite kingdom was disintegrating, further waves of Indo-European peoples migrated from southeastern Europe, which was followed by warfare.[[48]](#cite_note-49) It is not known if the [Trojan War](/wiki/Trojan_War "Trojan War") is based on historical events.[[49]](#cite_note-50) [Troy's Late Bronze Age](/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age_Troy "Late Bronze Age Troy") layers matches most with *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")*'s story.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

#### Post-Hittite Anatolia (12th–6th centuries BCE)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-Hittite Anatolia (12th–6th centuries BCE)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_theatre_of_ancient_Halicarnassus%2C_built_in_the_4th_century_BC_during_the_reign_of_King_Mausolos_and_enlarged_in_the_2nd_century_AD%2C_the_original_capacity_of_the_theatre_was_10%2C000%2C_Bodrum%2C_Turkey_%2816456817694%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_theatre_of_ancient_Halicarnassus,_built_in_the_4th_century_BC_during_the_reign_of_King_Mausolos_and_enlarged_in_the_2nd_century_AD,_the_original_capacity_of_the_theatre_was_10,000,_Bodrum,_Turkey_(16456817694).jpg)

The [Theatre at Halicarnassus](/wiki/Theatre_at_Halicarnassus "Theatre at Halicarnassus") (modern [Bodrum](/wiki/Bodrum "Bodrum")) was built in the 4th century BCE by [Mausolus](/wiki/Mausolus "Mausolus"), the [Persian](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") [satrap](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap") (governor) of [Caria](/wiki/Caria#Persian_satrapy "Caria"). The [Mausoleum at Halicarnassus](/wiki/Mausoleum_at_Halicarnassus "Mausoleum at Halicarnassus") was one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World").[[51]](#cite_note-history-52)[[52]](#cite_note-seven-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Ephesus_Celsus_Library_Fa%C3%A7ade.jpg/250px-Ephesus_Celsus_Library_Fa%C3%A7ade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ephesus_Celsus_Library_Fa%C3%A7ade.jpg)

The [Library of Celsus](/wiki/Library_of_Celsus "Library of Celsus") in [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus") was built by the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in 114–117.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The [Temple of Artemis](/wiki/Temple_of_Artemis "Temple of Artemis") in Ephesus, built by king [Croesus](/wiki/Croesus "Croesus") of [Lydia](/wiki/Lydia "Lydia") in the 6th century BC, was one of the [Seven Wonders of the Ancient World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Ancient_World "Seven Wonders of the Ancient World").[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Around 750 BC, [Phrygia](/wiki/Phrygia "Phrygia") had been established, with its two centers in [Gordium](/wiki/Gordium "Gordium") and modern-day [Kayseri](/wiki/Kayseri "Kayseri").[[55]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-2-56) [Phrygians](/wiki/Phrygians "Phrygians") spoke an Indo-European language, which was closer to [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") than Anatolian languages.[[56]](#cite_note-McMahon_Steadman_2012_p_522-57) Phrygians shared Anatolia with [Neo-Hittites](/wiki/Neo-Hittite_states "Neo-Hittite states") and [Urartu](/wiki/Urartu "Urartu"). Luwian-speakers were probably the majority in various Anatolian Neo-Hittite states.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Urartians spoke a non-Indo-European language and their capital was around [Lake Van](/wiki/Lake_Van "Lake Van").[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[55]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-2-56) Urartu and Phrygia fell in seventh century BC.[[55]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-2-56)[[59]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-60) They were replaced by [Carians](/wiki/Carians "Carians"), [Lycians](/wiki/Lycians "Lycians") and [Lydians](/wiki/Lydians "Lydians").[[59]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-60) These three cultures "can be considered a reassertion of the ancient, indigenous culture of the Hattian cities of Anatolia".[[59]](#cite_note-Howard_2016-60)

#### Early Greek presence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Early Greek presence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Afrodisias_-_Sebasti%C3%B3n_-_Sebasteion.jpg/330px-Afrodisias_-_Sebasti%C3%B3n_-_Sebasteion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afrodisias_-_Sebasti%C3%B3n_-_Sebasteion.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Tetrapil%C3%B3n_-_Afrodisias_-_02.jpg/250px-Tetrapil%C3%B3n_-_Afrodisias_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tetrapil%C3%B3n_-_Afrodisias_-_02.jpg)

The [Sebasteion](/wiki/Sebasteion "Sebasteion") (left) and [Tetrapylon](/wiki/Tetrapylon "Tetrapylon") (right) in [Aphrodisias](/wiki/Aphrodisias "Aphrodisias") of [Caria](/wiki/Caria "Caria"), which was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage Site List in 2017.

In the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), there were three[[60]](#cite_note-Bryce_2012_pp369–370-61) or four[[61]](#cite_note-Britannica-62) Greek-speaking settlements in Anatolia, including [Miletus](/wiki/Miletus "Miletus").[[60]](#cite_note-Bryce_2012_pp369–370-61)[[61]](#cite_note-Britannica-62) Around 1200–1000 BC, [Greeks started migrating](/wiki/Iron_Age_Greek_migrations "Iron Age Greek migrations") to the west coast of Anatolia.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) The size of these migrations are unknown.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Important [cities](/wiki/Polis "Polis") included [Miletus](/wiki/Miletus "Miletus"), [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus"), [Halicarnassus](/wiki/Halicarnassus "Halicarnassus"), [Smyrna](/wiki/Smyrna "Smyrna") (now [İzmir](/wiki/%C4%B0zmir "İzmir")) and others[[65]](#cite_note-66) These settlements were grouped as [Aeolis](/wiki/Aeolis "Aeolis"), [Ionia](/wiki/Ionia "Ionia"), and [Doris](/wiki/Doric_hexapolis "Doric hexapolis"), after the specific Greek groups that settled them.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) By 900 BC, Greek settlements emerged across the west coast of Anatolia from [Knidos](/wiki/Knidos "Knidos") in the south all the way to the entrance of the [Hellespont](/wiki/Hellespont "Hellespont") in the north.[[68]](#cite_note-69) In the Anatolian interior, Greek culture had limited influence until the time of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"); Greek settlements "largely (although not exclusively)" existed on the western coast of Anatolia in the early [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").[[69]](#cite_note-70)

Further Greek colonization in Anatolia was led by Miletus and [Megara](/wiki/Megara "Megara") in 750–480 BC, particularly in the [Sea of Marmara](/wiki/Sea_of_Marmara "Sea of Marmara") and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"); settlements were also established in the south coast of Anatolia, at [Phaselis](/wiki/Phaselis "Phaselis"), [Aspendos](/wiki/Aspendos "Aspendos"), and [Side](/wiki/Side,_Turkey "Side, Turkey").[[70]](#cite_note-71) Some archaeological evidence supports ancient Greeks' migration myths about the Ionian Migration not being a "virgin soil foundation", with a pre-Greek population present.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Aeolian and Ionian Greeks, refugees from their former Mycenaean kingdoms, mixed through marriages with native Anatolians.[[72]](#cite_note-73) In the late eighth century BC, the Greek cities of Anatolia maintained familial, linguistic, and religious connections to their close relatives on the Greek mainland. Around 750 BC, Greeks in Anatolia started to differentiate themselves from other Anatolians, whom they regarded as barbarians, through the concept of the [polis](/wiki/Polis "Polis").[[73]](#cite_note-74) The Greek city-states emerged in Anatolian western coasts as significant cultural and commercial hubs.[[74]](#cite_note-75) Their prosperity was based on extensive trade with the Anatolian peoples (Lycian, Lydian, Carian) and with other cities around the Aegean Sea.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarl2012755-76) They also made numerous achievements in philosophy, science, architecture, and literature.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarl2012755–761-77)

### Classical Anatolia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Classical Anatolia")]

Main article: [Classical Anatolia](/wiki/Classical_Anatolia "Classical Anatolia")

In [Classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), Anatolia was described by the Ancient Greek historian [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") and later historians as divided into regions that were diverse in culture, language, and religious practices.[[77]](#cite_note-yavuz-78) The northern regions included [Bithynia](/wiki/Bithynia "Bithynia"), [Paphlagonia](/wiki/Paphlagonia "Paphlagonia"), and [Pontus](/wiki/Pontus_(region) "Pontus (region)"); to the west were [Mysia](/wiki/Mysia "Mysia"), [Lydia](/wiki/Lydia "Lydia"), and Caria; and [Lycia](/wiki/Lycia "Lycia"), [Pamphylia](/wiki/Pamphylia "Pamphylia"), and [Cilicia](/wiki/Cilicia "Cilicia") belonged to the southern shore. There were also several inland regions: [Phrygia](/wiki/Phrygia "Phrygia"), [Cappadocia](/wiki/Cappadocia "Cappadocia"), [Pisidia](/wiki/Pisidia "Pisidia"), and [Galatia](/wiki/Galatia "Galatia").[[77]](#cite_note-yavuz-78) Languages spoken included the late surviving [Anatolic languages](/wiki/Anatolic_languages "Anatolic languages"), [Isaurian](/wiki/Isaurian_language "Isaurian language"),[[78]](#cite_note-79) and [Pisidian](/wiki/Pisidian_language "Pisidian language"), [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") in western and coastal regions, [Phrygian](/wiki/Phrygian_language "Phrygian language") spoken until the 7th century CE,[[79]](#cite_note-80) local variants of [Thracian](/wiki/Thracian "Thracian") in the northwest, the [Galatian variant of Gaulish](/wiki/Galatian_language "Galatian language") in [Galatia](/wiki/Galatia "Galatia") until the 6th century CE,[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) [Cappadocian](/wiki/Ancient_Cappadocian_language "Ancient Cappadocian language") in the homonymous region,[[83]](#cite_note-84) [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") in the east, and [Kartvelian languages](/wiki/Kartvelian_languages "Kartvelian languages") in the northeast.

Anatolia is known as the birthplace of minted [coinage](/wiki/Coin "Coin") (as opposed to unminted coinage, which first appears in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") at a much earlier date) as a medium of exchange, sometime in the 7th century BCE in Lydia. The use of minted coins continued to flourish during the [Greek](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") and [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") eras.[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

During the 6th century BCE, all of Anatolia was conquered by the [Persian](/wiki/Persia "Persia") [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), the Persians having usurped the [Medes](/wiki/Medes "Medes") as the [dominant dynasty of Persia](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Persia "List of monarchs of Persia"). In 499 BCE, the [Ionian](/wiki/Ionia "Ionia") city-states on the west coast of Anatolia rebelled against Persian rule. The [Ionian Revolt](/wiki/Ionian_Revolt "Ionian Revolt"), as it became known, though quelled, initiated the [Greco-Persian Wars](/wiki/Greco-Persian_Wars "Greco-Persian Wars"), which ended in a [Greek](/wiki/Delian_League "Delian League") victory in 449 BCE, and the Ionian cities regained their independence. By the [Peace of Antalcidas](/wiki/Peace_of_Antalcidas "Peace of Antalcidas") (387 BCE), which ended the [Corinthian War](/wiki/Corinthian_War "Corinthian War"), Persia regained control over Ionia.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)

#### Hellenistic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hellenistic period")]

In 334 BCE, the [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonia_(ancient_kingdom) "Macedonia (ancient kingdom)") Greek king [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") conquered the Anatolian peninsula from the Achaemenid Persian Empire.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Alexander restored democracy in the Greek cities of Anatolia and was greeted by them as a liberator. The Lydians and Carians also hailed Alexander.[[89]](#cite_note-90) Alexander's conquest opened up the interior of Asia Minor to Greek settlement and influence. Anatolian cities transformed themselves into Hellenic-style poleis, and cities in Caria, Lydia, Lycia and Cilicia adopted Greek language, culture and institutions.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Nemrut_Da%C4%9F%C4%B1_12.jpg/250px-Nemrut_Da%C4%9F%C4%B1_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nemrut_Da%C4%9F%C4%B1_12.jpg)

Sanctuary of the Kings of [Commagene](/wiki/Commagene "Commagene") on [Mount Nemrut](/wiki/Mount_Nemrut "Mount Nemrut") (1st century BCE)

Following the death of Alexander the Great and the subsequent breakup of the [Macedonian Empire](/wiki/Macedonian_Empire "Macedonian Empire"), Anatolia was ruled by a series of Hellenistic kingdoms, such as the [Attalids of Pergamum](/wiki/Attalid_dynasty "Attalid dynasty") and the [Seleucids](/wiki/Seleucids "Seleucids"), the latter controlling most of Anatolia. A period of peaceful [Hellenization](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization") followed, such that the local Anatolian languages had been supplanted by Greek by the 1st century BCE. In 133 BCE, the last Attalid king bequeathed his kingdom to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"); western and central Anatolia came under [Roman control](/wiki/Romanization_of_Anatolia "Romanization of Anatolia"), but [Hellenistic culture](/wiki/Hellenistic_culture "Hellenistic culture") remained predominant.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Asia_Minor_188_BCE.jpg/250px-Asia_Minor_188_BCE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asia_Minor_188_BCE.jpg)

Asia Minor in 188 BC

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/PonticKingdom.png/250px-PonticKingdom.png)](/wiki/File:PonticKingdom.png)

Kingdom of Pontus before the reign of Mithridates VI (120 BC, Dark Purple), after his early conquests (Light Purple), and his conquests in the first Mithridatic wars (88 BC, Pink). Armenia is in Green

[Mithridates VI Eupator](/wiki/Mithridates_VI_Eupator "Mithridates VI Eupator"), ruler of the [Kingdom of Pontus](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Pontus "Kingdom of Pontus") in northern Anatolia, waged war against the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in the year 88 BCE in order to halt the advance of Roman [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") in the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") region. Mithridates VI sought to dominate Asia Minor and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") region, waging several hard-fought but ultimately unsuccessful wars (the [Mithridatic Wars](/wiki/Mithridatic_Wars "Mithridatic Wars")) to break Roman dominion over Asia and the [Hellenic world](/wiki/Hellenic_world "Hellenic world").[[91]](#cite_note-92) He has been called the greatest ruler of the Kingdom of Pontus.[[92]](#cite_note-93) His ally and son-in-law, [Tigranes the Great](/wiki/Tigranes_the_Great "Tigranes the Great") of [Armenia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Armenia_(antiquity) "Kingdom of Armenia (antiquity)") (r. 95 – 55 BCE), briefly conquered significant portions of Anatolia, including Cilicia, Cappadocia, [Sophene](/wiki/Sophene "Sophene") and perhaps [Galatia](/wiki/Galatia "Galatia").[[93]](#cite_note-94) Further annexations by Rome, in particular of the Kingdom of Pontus by [Pompey](/wiki/Pompey "Pompey"), brought all of Anatolia under [Roman control](/wiki/Romanization_of_Anatolia "Romanization of Anatolia"), except for the southeastern frontier with the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire"), which remained unstable for centuries, causing a series of military conflicts that culminated in the [Roman–Parthian Wars](/wiki/Roman%E2%80%93Parthian_Wars "Roman–Parthian Wars") (54 BCE – 217 CE).

After the [first division of the Roman Empire](/wiki/First_division_of_the_Roman_Empire "First division of the Roman Empire"), Anatolia became part of the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), otherwise known as the Byzantine Empire or [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium").[[95]](#cite_note-Niewöhner_2017-96) In the 1st century CE, Anatolia became [one of the first places where Christianity spread](/wiki/History_of_early_Christianity "History of early Christianity"), so that by the 4th century CE, western and central Anatolia were overwhelmingly Christian and Greek-speaking.[[95]](#cite_note-Niewöhner_2017-96)

Byzantine Anatolia was one of the wealthiest and most densely populated places in the [Later Roman Empire](/wiki/Later_Roman_Empire "Later Roman Empire"). Anatolia's wealth grew during the 4th and 5th centuries thanks, in part, to the [Pilgrim's Road](/wiki/Pilgrim%27s_Road "Pilgrim's Road") that ran through the peninsula. Literary evidence about the rural landscape stems from the [Christian hagiographies](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography") of the 6th-century [Nicholas of Sion](/wiki/Nicholas_of_Sion "Nicholas of Sion") and 7th-century [Theodore of Sykeon](/wiki/Theodore_of_Sykeon "Theodore of Sykeon"). Large and prosperous urban centers of Byzantine Anatolia included [Assos](/wiki/Assos "Assos"), [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus"), [Miletus](/wiki/Miletus "Miletus"), [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea "Nicaea"), [Pergamum](/wiki/Pergamum "Pergamum"), [Priene](/wiki/Priene "Priene"), [Sardis](/wiki/Sardis "Sardis"), and [Aphrodisias](/wiki/Aphrodisias "Aphrodisias").[[95]](#cite_note-Niewöhner_2017-96)

From the mid-5th century onwards, urbanism was affected negatively and began to decline, while the rural areas reached unprecedented levels of prosperity in the region.[[95]](#cite_note-Niewöhner_2017-96) Historians and scholars continue to debate the cause of the urban decline in Byzantine Anatolia between the 6th and 7th centuries,[[95]](#cite_note-Niewöhner_2017-96) variously attributing it to the [Plague of Justinian](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian") (541), the [Byzantine–Sasanian War](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_War_of_602%E2%80%93628 "Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628") (602–628), and the [Arab invasion of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") (634–638).[[96]](#cite_note-thonemann-97)

### Medieval period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Medieval period")]

Further information: [Byzantine Anatolia](/wiki/Byzantine_Anatolia "Byzantine Anatolia"), [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), and [Sultanate of Rum](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Rum "Sultanate of Rum")

See also: [List of states in late medieval Anatolia](/wiki/List_of_states_in_late_medieval_Anatolia "List of states in late medieval Anatolia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Asia_Minor_ca_842_AD.svg/500px-Asia_Minor_ca_842_AD.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Asia_Minor_ca_842_AD.svg)

[Byzantine Anatolia](/wiki/Byzantine_Anatolia "Byzantine Anatolia") and the [Byzantine-Arab frontier zone](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Byzantine_wars "Arab–Byzantine wars") in the mid-9th century

In the 10 years following the [Battle of Manzikert](/wiki/Battle_of_Manzikert "Battle of Manzikert") in 1071, the [Seljuk Turks](/wiki/Seljuk_Turks "Seljuk Turks") from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") migrated over large areas of Anatolia, with particular concentrations around the northwestern rim.[[97]](#cite_note-98) The Turkish language and the Islamic religion were gradually introduced as a result of the Seljuk conquest, and this period marks the start of Anatolia's slow transition from predominantly Christian and Greek-speaking, to predominantly Muslim and Turkish-speaking (although ethnic groups such as Armenians, Greeks, and Assyrians remained numerous and retained Christianity and their native languages). In the following century, the [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Komnenos_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Komnenos dynasty") managed to reassert their control in western and northern Anatolia. Control of Anatolia was then split between the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and the Seljuk [Sultanate of Rûm](/wiki/Sultanate_of_R%C3%BBm "Sultanate of Rûm"), with the Byzantine holdings gradually being reduced.[[98]](#cite_note-Balyuzi-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/11_13th_century_Asia_Minor_Turkish_Invasions.png/250px-11_13th_century_Asia_Minor_Turkish_Invasions.png)](/wiki/File:11_13th_century_Asia_Minor_Turkish_Invasions.png)

Settlements and regions affected during the first wave of [Turkish invasions](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Seljuk_wars "Byzantine–Seljuk wars") in Asia Minor (11th–13th century)

The [Fourth Crusade](/wiki/Fourth_Crusade "Fourth Crusade") would prove to be a grave challenge to the Byzantine Empire. The loss of a central authority figure shattered the empire, breaking it into four major successor states that each held sway over different parts of the empire.[[99]](#cite_note-100) Two of these states, the [Empire of Nicaea](/wiki/Empire_of_Nicaea "Empire of Nicaea") and the [Empire of Trebizond](/wiki/Empire_of_Trebizond "Empire of Trebizond"), would contend for the remains of the empire in Anatolia, with the Nicaean Empire holding the northwestern coast and the Trapezuntine empire holding the northeastern coast.

In 1255, the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") swept through eastern and central Anatolia, and would remain until 1335. The [Ilkhanate](/wiki/Ilkhanate "Ilkhanate") garrison was stationed near [Ankara](/wiki/Ankara "Ankara").[[98]](#cite_note-Balyuzi-99)[[100]](#cite_note-101) After the decline of the Ilkhanate from 1335 to 1353, the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire")'s legacy in the region was the [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_people "Uyghur people") [Eretna Dynasty](/wiki/Eretna_Dynasty "Eretna Dynasty") that was overthrown by [Kadi Burhan al-Din](/wiki/Kadi_Burhan_al-Din "Kadi Burhan al-Din") in 1381.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

By the end of the 14th century, most of Anatolia was controlled by various [Anatolian beyliks](/wiki/Anatolian_beyliks "Anatolian beyliks"). Smyrna fell in 1330, and the last Byzantine stronghold in Anatolia, [Philadelphia](/wiki/Ala%C5%9Fehir "Alaşehir"), [fell in 1390](/wiki/Fall_of_Philadelphia "Fall of Philadelphia"). The [Turkmen](/wiki/Turkmen_people "Turkmen people") Beyliks were under the control of the Mongols, at least nominally, through declining Seljuk Sultans.[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104) The Beyliks did not mint coins in the names of their own leaders while they remained under the suzerainty of the [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") [Ilkhanids](/wiki/Ilkhanids "Ilkhanids").[[104]](#cite_note-105) The [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_dynasty "Ottoman dynasty") ruler [Osman I](/wiki/Osman_I "Osman I") was the first Turkish ruler who minted coins in his own name in 1320s; they bear the legend "Minted by Osman son of Ertugrul".[[105]](#cite_note-106) Since the minting of coins was a prerogative accorded in Islamic practice only to a [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty"), it can be considered that the Ottoman Turks had become formally independent from the Mongol Khans.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

### Ottoman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ottoman Empire")]

Further information: [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Growth_of_the_Ottoman_Empire.jpg/250px-Growth_of_the_Ottoman_Empire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Growth_of_the_Ottoman_Empire.jpg)

[Territorial evolution of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Territorial evolution of the Ottoman Empire") between 1359 and 1683

Among the [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") leaders, the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_dynasty "Ottoman dynasty") emerged as great power under [Osman I](/wiki/Osman_I "Osman I") and his son [Orhan](/wiki/Orhan "Orhan").[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109) The [Anatolian beyliks](/wiki/Anatolian_beyliks "Anatolian beyliks") were successively absorbed into the rising [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") during the 15th century.[[109]](#cite_note-110) It is not well understood how the Osmanlı, or [Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), came to dominate their neighbors, as the history of medieval Anatolia is still little known.[[110]](#cite_note-111) The Ottomans completed the conquest of the peninsula in 1517 with the taking of [Halicarnassus](/wiki/Halicarnassus "Halicarnassus") (modern [Bodrum](/wiki/Bodrum "Bodrum")) from the [Knights of Saint John](/wiki/Knights_of_Saint_John "Knights of Saint John").[[111]](#cite_note-112)

### Modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Modern times")]

Further information: [History of Turkey](/wiki/History_of_Turkey "History of Turkey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_Historical_Atlas%2C_1911_%E2%80%93_Distribution_of_Races_in_the_Balkan_Peninsula_and_Asia_Minor.jpg/250px-The_Historical_Atlas%2C_1911_%E2%80%93_Distribution_of_Races_in_the_Balkan_Peninsula_and_Asia_Minor.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Historical_Atlas,_1911_%E2%80%93_Distribution_of_Races_in_the_Balkan_Peninsula_and_Asia_Minor.jpg)

Ethnic map of Asia Minor in 1905–06

With the acceleration of the decline of the Ottoman Empire in the early 19th century, and as a result of the expansionist policies of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") in the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), many Muslim nations and groups in that region, mainly [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians "Circassians"), [Crimean Tatars](/wiki/Crimean_Tatars "Crimean Tatars"), [Azeris](/wiki/Azeris "Azeris"), [Lezgis](/wiki/Lezgian_people "Lezgian people"), [Chechens](/wiki/Chechens "Chechens"), [Muslim Georgians](/wiki/Chveneburi "Chveneburi"),[[112]](#cite_note-Incorporated2009-113) [Hamshenis](/wiki/Hemshin_people "Hemshin people")[[113]](#cite_note-114) and several [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") groups left their homelands and settled in Anatolia. As the Ottoman Empire further shrank in the [Balkan](/wiki/Balkan "Balkan") regions and then fragmented during the [Balkan Wars](/wiki/Balkan_Wars "Balkan Wars"), much of the non-Christian populations of its former possessions, mainly Balkan Muslims ([Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks"), [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians "Albanians"), [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people"), [Serb Muslims](/wiki/Serb_Muslims "Serb Muslims"),[[114]](#cite_note-Pekesen2012-115) [Muslim Bulgarians](/wiki/Pomaks "Pomaks") and [Greek Muslims](/wiki/Greek_Muslims "Greek Muslims") such as the [Vallahades](/wiki/Vallahades "Vallahades") from [Greek Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(Greece) "Macedonia (Greece)")), were resettled in various parts of Anatolia, mostly in formerly Christian villages throughout Anatolia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/St._Polycarp_Kilisesi_%282%29.jpg/250px-St._Polycarp_Kilisesi_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Polycarp_Kilisesi_(2).jpg)

St. Polycarp Church, in modern-day [Izmir](/wiki/%C4%B0zmir "İzmir").

A continuous reverse migration occurred since the early 19th century, when Greeks from Anatolia, [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and Pontus area migrated toward the newly independent [Kingdom of Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece"), and also towards the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the southern part of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), Latin America, and the rest of Europe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Ankara_and_mosque_wza.jpg/250px-Ankara_and_mosque_wza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ankara_and_mosque_wza.jpg)

Mosque in [Ankara](/wiki/Ankara "Ankara")

Following the Russo-Persian [Treaty of Turkmenchay](/wiki/Treaty_of_Turkmenchay "Treaty of Turkmenchay") (1828) and the incorporation of [Eastern Armenia](/wiki/Eastern_Armenia "Eastern Armenia") into the Russian Empire, another migration involved the large Armenian population of Anatolia, which recorded significant migration rates from [Western Armenia](/wiki/Western_Armenia "Western Armenia") (Eastern Anatolia) toward the Russian Empire, especially toward its newly established Armenian provinces.[[115]](#cite_note-Swietochowski_Borderland-116)

Anatolia remained [multi-ethnic](/wiki/Multi-ethnic "Multi-ethnic") until the early 20th century (see the [rise of nationalism under the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Rise_of_nationalism_under_the_Ottoman_Empire "Rise of nationalism under the Ottoman Empire")). During World War I, the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), the [Greek genocide](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide") (especially in [Pontus](/wiki/Pontus_(region) "Pontus (region)")), and the [Assyrian genocide](/wiki/Assyrian_genocide "Assyrian genocide") almost entirely removed the ancient indigenous communities of [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians"), [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), and [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") populations in Anatolia and surrounding regions. Following the [Greco-Turkish War of 1919–1922](/wiki/Greco-Turkish_War_of_1919%E2%80%931922 "Greco-Turkish War of 1919–1922"), most remaining ethnic Anatolian Greeks were forced out during the 1923 [population exchange between Greece and Turkey](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchange between Greece and Turkey"). Of the remainder, most have left Turkey since then, leaving fewer than 5,000 Greeks in Anatolia today.[[116]](#cite_note-117) According to Morris and Ze'evi, 4 million Christians were ethnically cleansed from Asia minor by the Turks from 1894 to 1924.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Lake_Tuz%2C_Turkey_%28Unsplash_O0uCm1WLnA0%29.jpg/250px-Lake_Tuz%2C_Turkey_%28Unsplash_O0uCm1WLnA0%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Tuz,_Turkey_(Unsplash_O0uCm1WLnA0).jpg)

Salty shores of [Lake Tuz](/wiki/Lake_Tuz "Lake Tuz")

Anatolia's terrain is structurally complex. A central [massif](/wiki/Massif "Massif") composed of uplifted blocks and downfolded [troughs](/wiki/Trough_(geology) "Trough (geology)"), covered by recent [deposits](/wiki/Deposit_(geology) "Deposit (geology)") and giving the appearance of a plateau with rough terrain, is wedged between two folded mountain ranges that converge in the east. True lowland is confined to a few narrow coastal strips along the Aegean, Mediterranean, and the Black Sea coasts. Flat or gently sloping land is rare and largely confined to the deltas of the [Kızıl River](/wiki/K%C4%B1z%C4%B1l_River "Kızıl River"), the coastal plains of [Çukurova](/wiki/%C3%87ukurova "Çukurova") and the valley floors of the [Gediz River](/wiki/Gediz_River "Gediz River") and the [Büyük Menderes River](/wiki/B%C3%BCy%C3%BCk_Menderes_River "Büyük Menderes River") as well as some interior high plains in Anatolia, mainly around [Lake Tuz](/wiki/Lake_Tuz "Lake Tuz") (Salt Lake) and the [Konya](/wiki/Konya "Konya") Basin (*Konya Ovasi*).

There are two mountain ranges in southern Anatolia: the [Taurus](/wiki/Taurus_Mountains "Taurus Mountains") and the [Zagros](/wiki/Zagros "Zagros") Mountains.[[118]](#cite_note-wiley-119)

### Climate

Anatolia has a varied range of climates. The central plateau is characterized by a continental climate, with hot summers and cold, snowy winters. The south and west coasts enjoy a typical Mediterranean climate, with mild rainy winters and warm, dry summers.[[119]](#cite_note-Prothero19-120) The Black Sea and Marmara coasts have a temperate oceanic climate, with warm, foggy summers and much rainfall throughout the year.

### Ecoregions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anatolia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ecoregions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Blue_Lagoon_-_2014.10_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Blue_Lagoon_-_2014.10_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blue_Lagoon_-_2014.10_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") is prevalent in the [Turkish Riviera](/wiki/Turkish_Riviera "Turkish Riviera")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Central_Anatolia_%2852397494465%29.jpg/250px-Central_Anatolia_%2852397494465%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Anatolia_(52397494465).jpg)

Anatolia's dry central plateau

There is a diverse number of plant and animal communities.

The mountains and coastal plain of northern Anatolia experience a humid and mild climate. There are [temperate broadleaf, mixed](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forest "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest") and [coniferous](/wiki/Temperate_coniferous_forest "Temperate coniferous forest") forests. The central and eastern plateau, with its drier [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate"), has deciduous forests and forest steppes. Western and southern Anatolia, which have a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), contain [Mediterranean forests, woodlands, and scrub](/wiki/Mediterranean_forests,_woodlands,_and_scrub "Mediterranean forests, woodlands, and scrub") ecoregions.

* [Euxine-Colchic deciduous forests](/wiki/Euxine-Colchic_deciduous_forests "Euxine-Colchic deciduous forests"): These temperate broadleaf and mixed forests extend across northern Anatolia, lying between the mountains of northern Anatolia and the Black Sea. They include the enclaves of [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest") lying along the southeastern coast of the Black Sea in eastern Turkey and Georgia.[[120]](#cite_note-121)
* [Northern Anatolian conifer and deciduous forests](/wiki/Northern_Anatolian_conifer_and_deciduous_forests "Northern Anatolian conifer and deciduous forests"): These forests occupy the mountains of northern Anatolia, running east and west between the coastal Euxine-Colchic forests and the drier, continental climate forests of central and eastern Anatolia.[[121]](#cite_note-122)
* [Central Anatolian deciduous forests](/wiki/Central_Anatolian_deciduous_forests "Central Anatolian deciduous forests"): These forests of deciduous oaks and evergreen pines cover the plateau of central Anatolia.[[122]](#cite_note-123)
* [Central Anatolian steppe](/wiki/Central_Anatolian_steppe "Central Anatolian steppe"): These dry grasslands cover the drier valleys and surround the saline lakes of central Anatolia, and include [halophytic](/wiki/Halophytic "Halophytic") (salt-tolerant) plant communities.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Pontic_Panorama.jpg/250px-Pontic_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontic_Panorama.jpg)

A panorama of the [Pontic Mountains](/wiki/Pontic_Mountains "Pontic Mountains") in the [Black Sea Region](/wiki/Black_Sea_Region "Black Sea Region") of northern Anatolia, Turkey

* [Eastern Anatolian deciduous forests](/wiki/Eastern_Anatolian_deciduous_forests "Eastern Anatolian deciduous forests"): This ecoregion occupies the plateau of eastern Anatolia. The drier and more continental climate is beneficial for steppe-forests dominated by deciduous oaks, with areas of shrubland, montane forest, and valley forest.[[124]](#cite_note-125)
* [Anatolian conifer and deciduous mixed forests](/wiki/Anatolian_conifer_and_deciduous_mixed_forests "Anatolian conifer and deciduous mixed forests"): These forests occupy the western, Mediterranean-climate portion of the Anatolian plateau. Pine forests, mixed pine, oak woodlands and shrublands are predominant.[[125]](#cite_note-126)
* [Aegean and Western Turkey sclerophyllous and mixed forests](/wiki/Aegean_and_Western_Turkey_sclerophyllous_and_mixed_forests "Aegean and Western Turkey sclerophyllous and mixed forests"): These Mediterranean-climate forests occupy the coastal lowlands and valleys of western Anatolia bordering the Aegean Sea. The ecoregion has forests of [Turkish pine](/wiki/Turkish_pine "Turkish pine") *(Pinus brutia)*, oak forests and woodlands, and [maquis shrubland](/wiki/Maquis_shrubland "Maquis shrubland") of Turkish pine and evergreen [sclerophyllous](/wiki/Sclerophyll "Sclerophyll") trees and shrubs, including [Olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive") *(Olea europaea)*, [Strawberry Tree](/wiki/Arbutus_unedo "Arbutus unedo") *(Arbutus unedo)*, *[Arbutus andrachne](/wiki/Arbutus_andrachne "Arbutus andrachne")*, Kermes Oak *([Quercus coccifera](/wiki/Quercus_coccifera "Quercus coccifera"))*, and [Bay Laurel](/wiki/Bay_Laurel "Bay Laurel") *(Laurus nobilis)*.[[126]](#cite_note-127)
* [Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests](/wiki/Southern_Anatolian_montane_conifer_and_deciduous_forests "Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests"): These mountain forests occupy the Mediterranean-climate [Taurus Mountains](/wiki/Taurus_Mountains "Taurus Mountains") of southern Anatolia. Conifer forests are predominant, chiefly Anatolian black pine *([Pinus nigra](/wiki/Pinus_nigra "Pinus nigra"))*, Cedar of Lebanon *([Cedrus libani](/wiki/Cedrus_libani "Cedrus libani"))*, Taurus fir *([Abies cilicica](/wiki/Abies_cilicica "Abies cilicica"))*, and [juniper](/wiki/Juniper "Juniper") *([Juniperus foetidissima](/wiki/Juniperus_foetidissima "Juniperus foetidissima")* and *[J. excelsa](/wiki/Juniperus_excelsa "Juniperus excelsa"))*. Broadleaf trees include oaks, [hornbeam](/wiki/Hornbeam "Hornbeam"), and [maples](/wiki/Maple "Maple").[[127]](#cite_note-128)
* [Eastern Mediterranean conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf forests](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean_conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf_forests "Eastern Mediterranean conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf forests"): This ecoregion occupies the coastal strip of southern Anatolia between the Taurus Mountains and the Mediterranean Sea. Plant communities include broadleaf sclerophyllous maquis shrublands, forests of Aleppo Pine *(*Pinus halepensis*)* and Turkish Pine *(Pinus brutia)*, and dry oak *(Quercus* spp.) woodlands and steppes.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

All have populations of more than 500,000.[[129]](#cite_note-130)
